An apparent-time bar chart: /r/ rates by age cohort with the change-in-progress arrow and an age-grading caution flag.
A dynamics triptych: levelling (local forms converging), new-dialect formation (mixture settling — the New Zealand case), and media spread (vocabulary only) as three labelled mechanism panels.

worked_example_blueprint.setup: A fieldwork dossier for the running narrative's district and its neighbouring valley: survey data for three features — the word for the well-bucket ('pail' upslope, 'bucket' downslope), rhoticity (older speakers pronounce final /r/, younger increasingly do not), and a grammatical form ('it wants oiled' in three upslope villages) — plus a perceptual sketch-map task where residents drew where 'the valley accent' begins. The task: run the dialectological toolkit end to end.
steps: Draw the isoglosses: plot the pail/bucket line, the 'wants oiled' area, and the (variable) rhoticity zone on the district sketch-map; observe that the three lines do not coincide — state the bundling principle and refuse to draw a single 'dialect border' the data doesn't support.

Read the continuum: village-by-village intelligibility is complete while the district's endpoints differ noticeably — connect to the language/dialect convention and the army-and-navy aphorism, and classify the district as a micro-continuum.

Apply apparent time to rhoticity: tabulate /r/ rates by age group (elders high, middle variable, young low) — infer change in progress toward non-rhoticity; add the validation caveat (age-grading versus change) and note what a real-time restudy in twenty years would settle.

Diagnose levelling: 'pail' is retreating among younger upslope speakers toward the regional 'bucket' — attribute to schooling and market-town contact (face-to-face), not media alone; contrast with a broadcast catchphrase all ages use: vocabulary via media, sound change via interaction.

Analyze the perceptual maps: residents place 'the valley accent' boundary at the old district line — where no isogloss bundle runs; state the finding: perceived boundaries track social geography and identity, and load the attitudes the sociolinguistics concept measured.

Close with the ideology audit: the schoolteacher calls upslope 'wants oiled' 'broken grammar' — answer with the toolkit: the form is regionally systematic, isogloss-mappable, historically ordinary — a dialect feature exactly as standard forms are; everyone in the district, teacher included, speaks a mapped variety.
worked_example_blueprint.expected_outcome: The learner plots non-coinciding isoglosses and refuses unsupported borders, reads a continuum correctly, applies apparent time with its caveat, distinguishes levelling mechanisms from media vocabulary spread, interprets perceptual maps as attitude data, and dissolves a 'broken grammar' judgment with the field's instruments.

item_types: Isogloss-mapping task (plot feature boundaries from survey tables and assess bundling)
Apparent-time interpretation task (read age-stratified data as change in progress, stating the age-grading caveat)
Dynamics-diagnosis task (classify scenarios as levelling, new-dialect formation, or media vocabulary spread, with mechanism named)
practice_blueprint.difficulty_progression: Begin with single-feature isogloss plotting; progress to multi-feature bundling judgments and apparent-time tables; end with integrated dossiers combining maps, age data, perceptual sketches, and one ideology audit to be answered with evidence.

assessment_blueprint.bloom_alignment: Analyze — learners must decompose survey data into feature geographies, infer change from age stratification, and attribute contemporary dynamics to their mechanisms.
assessment_blueprint.mastery_signal: The learner draws only data-supported boundaries, reads apparent time with the validation caveat, separates face-to-face from media transmission channels, treats perceptual maps as attitude evidence, and applies the everyone-speaks-a-dialect principle to judgment cases.

real_world_applications: Forensic speaker profiling draws on dialect geography — feature bundles can indicate a speaker's regional background, within the field's carefully stated limits.
Speech technology must handle regional variation: recognizers trained on one variety fail others, and dialect-aware modelling is an active engineering requirement.
Marketing, dubbing, and localization use dialect maps operationally — which lexical variant, which accent for which market — and perceptual-dialectology findings predict audience reactions.

source_references: Fromkin, Rodman & Hyams An Introduction to Language
Yule, G. The Study of Language

assets.learning_objective: Analyze bilingualism at individual and societal levels: characterize bilingual competence against the two-monolinguals myth, evaluate cognitive and social findings with their debates stated honestly, and analyze societal multilingualism — diglossia, language maintenance and shift, and endangerment — with its policy stakes.
assets.concept_summary: Bilingualism and multilingualism — command and use of more than one language — are the human norm: most of the world's population uses multiple languages routinely, and the monolingual default assumed by much early research is the special case. At the individual level, the founding correction is Grosjean's principle that a bilingual is not two monolinguals in one person: bilinguals acquire and use their languages in different domains, for different purposes, with proficiency profiles that differ by skill (a speaker may read best in one language, joke best in another) — so measuring each language against a monolingual yardstick systematically misdescribes the competence. Bilinguals' languages are simultaneously active (both systems show activation even in single-language settings, a robust psycholinguistic finding), which makes code-switching — analyzed in the sociolinguistics concept as grammatically constrained and socially strategic — the natural behaviour of a doubly-live system rather than interference. Types are distinguished by onset and route: simultaneous bilingualism (two languages from infancy — children separate the systems early, and mixing is developmentally ordinary, not confusion) versus sequential (a second language after the first is established, connecting to the acquisition concept's interlanguage and critical-period findings). On cognition, the survey reports honestly: bilingual children reliably show earlier metalinguistic awareness (understanding that names are conventions — the domain's arbitrariness feature, discovered early by children who own two names for one thing); claims of a general executive-function 'bilingual advantage' generated an enormous literature and an ongoing replication debate, and the responsible summary is contested-and-open; what is settled is that bilingualism carries no cognitive cost — the deficit view that shaped early twentieth-century testing and policy is decisively refuted. At the societal level: multilingual societies organize their languages functionally — Ferguson's diglossia names the stable arrangement where a High variety serves formal/written domains and a Low variety everyday talk (Arabic's standard/colloquial pairing; Swiss German alongside Standard German) — and communities undergo language maintenance or shift across generations, with the canonical three-generation pattern in migration settings (monolingual grandparents, bilingual parents, majority-language-dominant grandchildren). At the far end of shift lies endangerment: roughly half the world's ~7,000 languages are at risk, intergenerational transmission is the diagnostic variable (Fishman's insight that schools and media cannot substitute for the home), and revitalization ranges from documentation through immersion schooling to the benchmark case of Hebrew's revernacularization — with Māori and Welsh among the instructive partial successes. The policy stakes connect back to applied linguistics: additive versus subtractive schooling decides whether children's home languages are resources kept or costs discarded.
key_ideas: Multilingualism is the global norm; the monolingual baseline is the special case, and research design has had to correct for it.
A bilingual is not two monolinguals (Grosjean): proficiency is domain- and skill-differentiated, and both languages remain active even in single-language use.
Simultaneous bilingual children separate their systems early — mixing is developmentally ordinary; sequential bilingualism runs through interlanguage and the critical-period gradient.

Cognitive findings, stated honestly: earlier metalinguistic awareness is robust; the executive-function 'bilingual advantage' is contested and unresolved; cognitive-deficit claims are decisively refuted.
Societies organize multilingualism functionally: diglossia (Ferguson's High/Low domains), and communities maintain or shift languages — canonically across three migration generations.

Endangerment threatens roughly half of the world's languages; intergenerational home transmission is the decisive variable (Fishman), and revitalization outcomes range from Hebrew's success through Māori and Welsh's partial gains.

common_misconceptions.misconception: Raising a child with two languages causes confusion and delays language development.
common_misconceptions.correction: Simultaneous bilinguals hit the same milestones on the same schedule (vocabulary totals across both languages match monolingual totals), separate their systems early, and their mixing is ordinary developmental behaviour that resolves — the same verdict acquisition research gave code-switching in adults: competence, not confusion. The confusion claim descends from discredited early testing, not evidence.

common_misconceptions.misconception: A real bilingual speaks both languages perfectly and equally — anything less is failed bilingualism.
common_misconceptions.correction: Balanced-and-perfect is the two-monolinguals myth in disguise: actual bilinguals distribute their languages across domains and skills by the lives they lead, and profiles shift over a lifetime. Bilingual competence is the integrated repertoire doing its jobs, and measuring each half against a monolingual yardstick misses the whole.

common_misconceptions.misconception: The bilingual cognitive advantage is settled science — bilingualism makes you smarter.
common_misconceptions.correction: The honest report is layered: earlier metalinguistic awareness is well supported; the broader executive-function advantage is the subject of a live replication debate with evidence on both sides; and 'no cognitive cost' is settled. Overselling the advantage invites the same evidential carelessness as the old deficit claims — the field's credibility lies in reporting the layers.

common_misconceptions.misconception: Endangered languages die because parents freely choose a more useful language — schools and apps can save them anyway.
common_misconceptions.correction: Shift typically happens under economic and institutional pressure and stigma — the 'choice' is structured — and the revitalization evidence (Fishman's) is that nothing substitutes for intergenerational home transmission: schools and media support it but cannot replace it. Where communities re-secured the home domain (Hebrew; Māori language nests), revitalization gained ground; where efforts stayed institutional, it mostly did not.

visual_teaching_suggestions: A repertoire wheel for one bilingual: domains as sectors (home, school, market, worship, jokes) shaded by language — the two-monolinguals myth refuted in one image.
A diglossia domain map: High and Low columns with the district's functions sorted, and arrows marking domain intrusions as instability signals.
A three-generation transmission diagram: grandparent/parent/child language profiles with the home-transmission link highlighted as the variable revitalization must secure.

worked_example_blueprint.setup: A three-generation dossier from the running narrative's district, where the local language (call it Valley) coexists with English: Tesfay's generation is Valley-dominant with formal English; his daughter runs her shop bilingually, switching by customer and topic; the granddaughter (the acquisition concept's child) grows up simultaneous bilingual, jokes in Valley, does arithmetic in English, and her school proposes English-only schooling 'to avoid confusion'. District-wide: sermons and law in English, market and home in Valley; village elders note fewer children speak Valley each decade. The task: analyze individual profiles, the societal arrangement, and the shift trajectory, with recommendations.
steps: Profile the granddaughter against the two-monolinguals myth: domain-differentiated skills (humour in Valley, arithmetic in English) are the normal bilingual signature — assess her by total repertoire and reject the school's confusion premise with the simultaneous-acquisition evidence (on-schedule milestones, early system separation).

Analyze the mother's shop behaviour: switching by customer (solidarity) and topic (technical stock terms in English) — apply the sociolinguistics concept's verdict (constrained, strategic) and add the psycholinguistic layer: both languages are always active; switching is the doubly-live system managed, not leakage.

Name the societal arrangement: English in sermons, law, and school versus Valley in home and market is diglossia-like functional allocation — map the High/Low domains and note the stability condition (each variety secure in its domains) and the instability sign (English entering home domains via schooling).

Read the shift diagnostics: elders' observation plus the school proposal constitute the three-generation pattern mid-flight — grandparents Valley-monolingual, parents bilingual, children at risk of English dominance; identify intergenerational home transmission as the variable to watch, per Fishman.

Evaluate the school proposal with applied-linguistics evidence: English-only schooling would be subtractive — undermining Valley's home transmission while (per medium-of-instruction findings) serving English outcomes worse than additive bilingual schooling; recommend home-language early literacy with planned English addition, and Valley domains protected.

Close with the honest-cognition brief for the district officer: no cost (settled), metalinguistic gains (robust), executive advantage (contested — do not oversell); the case for the granddaughter's bilingualism rests on identity, repertoire, and rights as much as on any cognitive bonus.
worked_example_blueprint.expected_outcome: The learner profiles bilinguals by repertoire rather than monolingual yardsticks, integrates the switching and activation findings, maps a diglossic allocation and its instability signs, diagnoses three-generation shift with transmission as the key variable, and issues evidence-calibrated recommendations including the honestly-layered cognition brief.

item_types: Profile-analysis task (given bilingual case descriptions, characterize domain/skill distribution and critique monolingual-yardstick assessments)
Societal-arrangement task (map High/Low domain allocations, classify stability, and diagnose maintenance versus shift from generational data)
Evidence-calibration task (sort claims about bilingual cognition and revitalization into settled, robust, contested, and refuted, with the study logic named)
practice_blueprint.difficulty_progression: Begin with individual profile analyses against the two-monolinguals myth; progress to societal mapping and three-generation diagnostics; end with integrated policy scenarios requiring calibrated evidence briefs and additive-schooling recommendations.

assessment_blueprint.bloom_alignment: Analyze — learners must decompose bilingual competence into domain profiles, societal multilingualism into functional allocations and generational trajectories, and the research literature into evidence tiers.
assessment_blueprint.mastery_signal: The learner never measures bilinguals against monolingual yardsticks, reads mixing and switching as competence, maps diglossic domains with stability analysis, applies the three-generation and transmission diagnostics, and reports the cognition literature in honest layers.

real_world_applications: Family language planning is a live decision for millions of households; the simultaneous-acquisition and transmission evidence directly informs what parents and pediatricians advise.
Education systems serving multilingual populations decide additive versus subtractive designs at scale — the dossier's school question is the daily reality of ministries worldwide.
Language revitalization is an active global movement (immersion nests, documentation projects, media in minority languages), and its resource allocation turns on the transmission-first evidence.

source_references: Fromkin, Rodman & Hyams An Introduction to Language
Yule, G. The Study of Language

assets.learning_objective: Analyze translation as decision-making under constraint: apply the equivalence spectrum from literal to dynamic, diagnose classic translation problems (idioms, culture-bound terms, register, untranslatables), distinguish translation from interpreting, and evaluate machine translation's capabilities and limits.
assets.concept_summary: Translation studies examines the theory and practice of transferring meaning between languages — and its founding insight is that transfer is never mechanical substitution, because languages do not partition meaning identically. The organizing instrument is the equivalence spectrum. At one end, formal (literal) equivalence tracks the source's words and structures as closely as the target language permits; at the other, dynamic (functional) equivalence — the terms are Eugene Nida's, from the Bible-translation tradition that founded much of the field — seeks the same effect on the target reader that the source had on its readers. Neither end is 'correct': the right position depends on the text's function, a principle German functionalists generalized as skopos theory — the translation's purpose governs its strategy (a court document, a poem, and an advertising slogan for the same product demand different equivalences). The spectrum becomes vivid at the classic problem sites. Idioms: 'kick the bucket' translated word-for-word produces nonsense — the semantics concept's non-compositionality made practical; the translator must find the target idiom with the same function, not the same imagery. Culture-bound terms: words like English 'privacy', Danish 'hygge', or kinship systems that split where English lumps (many languages distinguish maternal from paternal uncles) lack ready equivalents — strategies range from borrowing and calquing to explanatory paraphrase, each with costs. Register and dialect: a source text's formality, politeness calibration (the pragmatics concept's face-work, which languages grammaticalize differently — tu/vous distinctions English lacks), and dialect voices force choices with no neutral option. 'Untranslatability' is thus better stated precisely: anything sayable in one language is expressible in another (the expressibility principle), but not always with the same compactness, connotation, and form — poetry, where sound and meaning fuse (the literature domain's meter and rhyme), marks the practical limit, which is why verse translation is called re-creation. Interpreting — real-time oral translation, simultaneous or consecutive — adds cognitive constraints translation escapes: memory load, speed, and no revision, a genuinely distinct professional skill. Machine translation completes the survey: from rule-based systems through statistical to neural MT, quality has risen dramatically on high-resource language pairs, yet the failure modes track this concept's problem sites exactly — idioms, register, culture-bound terms, low-resource languages — and professional practice has reorganized around post-editing, with the human translator as quality authority. The comparative-literature concept's discipline holds here as the consumer's rule: every translation is an interpretive performance, and comparing two translations of one passage reveals both the original's possibilities and each translator's decisions.
key_ideas: Translation is decision-making under constraint, not substitution: languages partition meaning differently, so every rendering selects.
The equivalence spectrum runs from formal (structure-tracking) to dynamic (effect-matching) — Nida's distinction — and skopos (purpose) decides the right position per text.

Classic problem sites: idioms (non-compositional, translate by function), culture-bound terms (borrow, calque, or paraphrase — each with costs), and register/politeness systems languages grammaticalize differently.
Untranslatability, stated precisely: everything is expressible across languages, but not always with equal compactness, connotation, or form — poetry marks the practical limit.
Interpreting is translation under real-time cognitive constraint (simultaneous/consecutive) — a distinct skill with no revision pass.
Neural machine translation is strong on high-resource pairs and fails precisely at this concept's problem sites; professional practice centres on human post-editing and quality authority.

common_misconceptions.misconception: Translation is word substitution — a big enough dictionary solves it.
common_misconceptions.correction: Word-for-word rendering breaks on the first idiom, the first register choice, the first term the target language splits differently. Translators translate texts-in-purpose, not words: the unit of fidelity is function and meaning in context, which is why the same sentence translates differently in a contract and a novel.

common_misconceptions.misconception: A good translation is always the most literal one — departures are liberties.
common_misconceptions.correction: Literalism is one strategy on a spectrum, right for some purposes (legal parallel texts, scholarly cribs) and wrong for others: the literal rendering of an idiom is a mistranslation of its meaning, and the literal politeness form can be a register error. Skopos decides: fidelity to function sometimes requires formal departure.

common_misconceptions.misconception: Some things simply cannot be translated — untranslatable words prove languages are sealed worlds.
common_misconceptions.correction: The expressibility principle holds: any meaning can be conveyed in any language, though perhaps by phrase rather than word and with connotative and formal losses. 'Untranslatable' words are compactness gaps, not meaning walls — 'hygge' takes English a sentence instead of a syllable. The real limit case is form-meaning fusion (verse), where translation becomes acknowledged re-creation.

common_misconceptions.misconception: Machine translation has solved translation — human translators are obsolete.
common_misconceptions.correction: Neural MT is impressively fluent on high-resource pairs and routine text, but its failures cluster exactly at the field's known problem sites — idioms rendered literally, register flattened, culture-bound terms mishandled, low-resource languages poorly served — and fluent output masks errors, raising the stakes. The profession has shifted toward post-editing and quality authority, not disappearance; for consequential texts, human judgment remains the standard.

visual_teaching_suggestions: An equivalence-slider diagram: formal to dynamic, with the four worked texts pinned at their skopos-determined positions.
A problem-site board: idiom, culture-bound term, register, verse — each with a literal rendering, a functional rendering, and the documented trade-off between them.
An MT-audit checklist card: predicted failure sites down one side, verification and post-edit columns beside — the professional workflow as a one-page instrument.

worked_example_blueprint.setup: Materials from the running narrative, to be rendered from English into a target language the learner works with (or analyzed via provided glosses): (1) the sign Amara posts — 'Water is under lock and key until the rains' (idiom); (2) the district's legal notice — 'The undersigned hereby covenants...' (register); (3) Tesfay's gate line — 'Kind of you to keep it safe for us' (irony and politeness); (4) the drought haiku from the literature domain (verse). The task: translate or analyze each under an assigned skopos, then compare with machine output.
steps: Set the skopos per text before translating: (1) public sign — clarity and tone for neighbours; (2) legal notice — formal precision, parallel-text accountability; (3) dialogue in a story — character voice and the irony's survival; (4) poem — re-creation with the cut and image intact; state how each purpose positions the slider on the equivalence spectrum.

Translate the idiom: render 'under lock and key' word-for-word into the target and observe the damage; then find the target language's own security idiom or a plain-function equivalent — document the decision as function-over-imagery, the semantics concept's non-compositionality applied.

Translate the legal register: preserve performative force ('hereby covenants') with the target's legal formula, noting where the target grammaticalizes formality English marks lexically (tu/vous-type choices, honorifics) — a decision English's source text never had to make, demonstrating that translation can force choices the original left open.

Translate the irony: keep Tesfay's line's surface praise while ensuring the target's politeness conventions let the accusation read through — test by back-translation whether both layers survived; if the target's conventions make the sarcasm invisible or too blunt, adjust and record the trade-off.

Attempt the haiku and mark the limit: preserve image and cut; log what is lost (syllable shape, sound texture) — classify the result as re-creation and connect to the comparative-literature concept's translation-comparison discipline.

Run all four through a neural MT system and audit: predict the failure sites first (idiom literalized? register flattened? irony lost?), verify against output, and write the post-editing brief — the professional workflow enacted, with the human as quality authority.
worked_example_blueprint.expected_outcome: The learner sets skopos before strategy, translates function over form at the problem sites, documents forced choices and trade-offs, marks the verse limit honestly as re-creation, and audits machine output against predicted failure modes with a post-editing brief.

item_types: Strategy-selection task (given texts and purposes, position the equivalence slider and justify)
Problem-site translation task (idioms, culture-bound terms, register items — translate or evaluate renderings with the decision documented)
MT-audit task (predict failure sites for a passage, compare machine output, and write the post-editing corrections)
practice_blueprint.difficulty_progression: Begin with strategy selection on contrasting text types; progress to problem-site work with documented decisions and back-translation checks; end with full skopos-to-post-editing workflows on mixed texts including one verse limit case.

assessment_blueprint.bloom_alignment: Evaluate — learners must judge renderings against purpose, weigh competing losses at problem sites, and appraise machine output as quality authorities.
assessment_blueprint.mastery_signal: The learner states skopos before strategy, translates idioms by function, documents forced choices (register, politeness) explicitly, states untranslatability precisely as compactness-and-form loss, and audits MT output at the predicted failure sites rather than trusting fluency.

real_world_applications: Localization is a global industry — software, games, marketing, and legal compliance all run skopos-driven translation workflows with post-editing at scale.

Community and public-service interpreting (hospitals, courts, asylum hearings) is high-stakes daily practice where the translation/interpreting distinction and register fidelity carry legal and medical consequences.
Every reader of translated news, literature, and scripture is a translation consumer; comparing two renderings of one passage is the practical literacy this concept installs.

source_references: Fromkin, Rodman & Hyams An Introduction to Language
Yule, G. The Study of Language

assets.learning_objective: Use corpora — large, principled collections of authentic text — to study language empirically: read concordances and collocation statistics, compare frequencies across registers with normalization, respect representativeness limits, and state what corpus evidence can and cannot settle.
assets.concept_summary: Corpus linguistics studies language through corpora: large, structured collections of authentic text and transcribed speech, sampled on principle and queried by software. It is a methodology serving every level of the domain rather than a level of its own — and its founding demonstration is that intuition about usage is systematically unreliable: speakers confidently misreport how often they use words, which senses dominate, and which combinations are normal, while corpus counts settle such questions in seconds. The toolkit's core instruments: the concordance (every occurrence of a query word displayed in context — KWIC, 'key word in context' — turning reading into pattern-scanning across hundreds of instances); frequency lists (raw counts, meaningful only after normalization to a common base such as per million words when corpora differ in size); collocation statistics (measures like mutual information and log-likelihood that separate a word's habitual partners from chance co-occurrence — the vocabulary domain's collocations, now measurable: 'strong tea' but 'powerful engine', and around the running specimen, 'unlock' partnering 'door', 'gate', 'potential', 'phone'); and register comparison (the same feature counted across sub-corpora of conversation, fiction, news, and academic prose — the design of major reference corpora from the pioneering million-word Brown Corpus of 1961 through the 100-million-word British National Corpus — revealing, in Douglas Biber's tradition of register studies, that grammar varies by situation as systematically as vocabulary: passives cluster in academic prose, contractions in conversation). Two disciplines govern inference. Representativeness: a corpus is evidence about the language varieties it samples — a newspaper corpus licenses claims about newspaper English, and 'the corpus shows X' silently means 'in these registers, in this period'. And the absence caveat: corpora prove presence and measure frequency, but absence proves little (rare ≠ impossible — the syntax concept's grammaticality judgments remain a distinct evidence type that corpus data complements rather than replaces). The method's reach is wide: lexicography rebuilt itself on corpora (modern dictionaries rank senses and choose examples by corpus evidence); language teaching draws frequency-informed syllabi and data-driven learning (students reading concordances directly); historical corpora track change quantitatively (the historical concept's semantic pathways, now countable decade by decade); and forensic and stylistic applications compare disputed texts against reference norms. Corpus linguistics is the domain's collect-pattern-rule loop industrialized — and the bridge to the computational concept that closes the domain, where the same corpora train language technology.
key_ideas: A corpus is a principled sample of authentic language queried by software — and intuition about usage is demonstrably unreliable where corpus counts are decisive.
The concordance (KWIC) turns hundreds of contexts into scannable pattern evidence; frequency comparisons require normalization to a common base.
Collocation statistics separate habitual partnerships ('strong tea') from chance co-occurrence, making the vocabulary domain's collocations measurable.

Register comparison across sub-corpora (conversation, fiction, news, academic) shows grammar varying by situation as systematically as vocabulary (Biber's tradition; Brown and BNC as landmark designs).
Representativeness bounds every claim: a corpus evidences the varieties it samples, and 'the corpus shows' means 'in these registers, this period'.
Corpora prove presence and frequency, not impossibility: absence is weak evidence, and grammaticality judgments remain a complementary data type.

common_misconceptions.misconception: Native-speaker intuition already knows how the language is used — corpora just confirm it expensively.
common_misconceptions.correction: Intuition is reliable for grammaticality but demonstrably poor at frequency and distribution: speakers misestimate common senses, miss their own most-used collocations, and misreport register differences. Corpus counts routinely overturn confident introspection — which is precisely why lexicography and testing rebuilt themselves on corpus evidence.

common_misconceptions.misconception: Bigger is always better — the largest corpus wins.
common_misconceptions.correction: Design beats raw size for most questions: a claim about conversation needs conversational data however large a news crawl is, and an unbalanced giant licenses only claims about what it contains. Size buys statistical power for rare items; representativeness decides what the power is evidence about — the two virtues are independent.

common_misconceptions.misconception: If a form doesn't occur in the corpus, it isn't part of the language.
common_misconceptions.correction: Absence conflates the impossible with the merely rare and the unsampled: perfectly grammatical sentences have zero corpus frequency (productivity guarantees it — most possible sentences have never been uttered). Corpora measure what happens, not what can happen; possibility questions still need judgment data.

common_misconceptions.misconception: Corpus work is just counting words — it can't touch grammar or meaning.
common_misconceptions.correction: Annotated corpora tag parts of speech and parse structure, enabling grammatical queries (passive rates by register, relative-clause types); concordance sense-sorting quantifies meaning distinctions; and collocation profiles distinguish near-synonyms ('strong'/'powerful') no definition separates cleanly. Counting is the entry point; the queries reach every level of the domain.

visual_teaching_suggestions: A KWIC screenshot-style panel for 'unlock' with the object column highlighted and sorted, physical and abstract senses colour-coded — concordance reading as a visible skill.
A normalization before/after chart: raw register counts versus per-million rates for one word, with the reversed ranking as the lesson.
A collocation profile card: 'unlock' versus 'open' as two labelled partner-clouds, MI-heavy partners in one ring, log-likelihood-heavy in another.

worked_example_blueprint.setup: A corpus session on the running specimen's verb: 'unlock' queried in a balanced reference corpus (conversation, fiction, news, academic sub-corpora), with (1) a KWIC concordance of 300 hits, (2) frequency tables raw and per-million by register, (3) collocation rankings by mutual information and log-likelihood, and (4) a comparison query for 'open' — plus one trap: a claim that 'unlock NP potential' is 'not real English' because a small corpus lacks it. The task: run the full corpus workflow and adjudicate the trap.
steps: Scan the concordance: sort the 300 KWIC lines by right context and induce the object profile — physical objects (door, gate, cell) versus abstract objects (potential, value, opportunity, phone) — turning raw hits into a sense-and-pattern map by systematic scanning, not anecdote.

Normalize the register table: raw counts mislead (the news sub-corpus is largest); convert to per-million and read the real distribution — the abstract 'unlock potential/value' pattern concentrating in news and marketing-adjacent prose, the physical sense spread evenly; state the register finding in Biber's terms: the word's senses have situational homes.

Read the collocation statistics: high mutual-information partners (padlock, potential) versus high log-likelihood partners (door, gate) — explain the instruments: MI rewards exclusivity (rare-but-faithful pairs), log-likelihood rewards reliable frequency; both together profile the word's company.

Contrast with 'open': overlapping objects (door, gate) but divergent profiles ('open' takes meetings, accounts, fire) — demonstrate collocation profiling as the tool that separates near-synonyms where definitions blur, the vocabulary domain's intuition made measurable.

Adjudicate the trap: the small corpus lacking 'unlock potential' shows sampling, not impossibility — the balanced corpus has hundreds of instances; state both disciplines: representativeness (the small corpus's registers didn't host the pattern) and the absence caveat (zero frequency ≠ ungrammatical), and note the converse check: presence in the large corpus settles the 'not real English' claim decisively.

Close with the loop made industrial: the session executed collect (corpus) → pattern (concordance, collocation) → rule (sense-register profile) → test (the 'open' contrast, the trap) — the domain's founding method, now at a scale no unaided reader could match.
worked_example_blueprint.expected_outcome: The learner reads concordances into pattern maps, normalizes before comparing registers, interprets MI and log-likelihood as complementary collocation lenses, separates near-synonyms by profile, and adjudicates an absence claim with both representativeness and the absence caveat stated.

item_types: Concordance-reading task (induce sense and pattern profiles from KWIC displays)
Normalization and register-comparison task (convert raw tables to per-million and interpret distributional findings)
Inference-audit task (evaluate claims made from corpus data for representativeness and absence-caveat violations)
practice_blueprint.difficulty_progression: Begin with guided concordance scanning on single words; progress to normalized register comparisons and collocation-measure interpretation; end with inference audits and a small self-designed query answering one usage question with disciplined conclusions.

assessment_blueprint.bloom_alignment: Apply — learners must operate the corpus toolkit on authentic data and produce disciplined findings, executing the method rather than describing it.
assessment_blueprint.mastery_signal: The learner never compares unnormalized counts, reads concordances systematically rather than anecdotally, interprets collocation statistics correctly, and bounds every conclusion with representativeness and the absence caveat unprompted.

real_world_applications: Modern dictionaries and grammars are corpus products: sense ranking, example selection, and usage notes all follow corpus evidence — every dictionary user consumes this method's output.
Language teaching and testing use frequency-informed word lists and data-driven learning; exam boards validate item language against reference corpora.
Search, autocomplete, and writing assistants run on corpus statistics, and forensic stylistics compares disputed documents against reference norms — corpus method as infrastructure and evidence.

source_references: Fromkin, Rodman & Hyams An Introduction to Language
Yule, G. The Study of Language

assets.learning_objective: Survey computational approaches to language: trace the field's arc from rules through statistics to neural models, explain core tasks and the ambiguity problem that unifies them, describe how n-gram and neural language models work in principle, and evaluate modern systems' capabilities and limits with the domain's linguistic instruments.
assets.concept_summary: Computational linguistics — with its engineering face, natural language processing (NLP) — studies how to process and model human language computationally. The survey closes the domain because it inherits everything: the levels tower returns as the field's task list — speech recognition and synthesis (phonetics/phonology), morphological analysis and stemming (morphology), part-of-speech tagging and parsing (syntax), word-sense disambiguation and semantic similarity (semantics), dialogue management (pragmatics/discourse), and machine translation (the previous concept's application flagship). One problem unifies them: ambiguity, everywhere the domain found it — the syntax concept's attachment ambiguities ('She watched the man with the telescope') are a parser's daily fare, the garden paths of psycholinguistics are an incremental system's hazard, and resolving ambiguity requires exactly the frequency, plausibility, and context information humans deploy. The field's history is three eras. Rule-based systems (from the 1950s) hand-coded grammars and lexicons: linguistically transparent, brittle against real text's variety — and the era's cautionary emblem is the early machine-translation overpromise that ended in the funding winter of the 1960s. Statistical NLP (from the late 1980s) learned probabilities from corpora — the previous concept's data industrialized: n-gram language models estimate each word's probability from the preceding few words (bigrams, trigrams), enough to power the era's speech recognizers and translators, with hidden Markov models tagging parts of speech; the limitation is horizon — an n-gram cannot see past its window, missing long-distance dependencies the syntax concept's recursion guarantees. Neural language models (dominant from the 2010s) replaced symbol-counting with learned vector representations: word embeddings place words in a space where distributional neighbours ('lock', 'key', 'bolt') cluster — the distributional hypothesis, 'you shall know a word by the company it keeps' (Firth), which is the corpus concept's collocation insight become geometry; sequence models and then transformer architectures with attention mechanisms learn long-range dependencies at scale, and today's large language models, trained on vast corpora to predict the next word, generate fluent text and perform many of the classical tasks at once. The survey's evaluative discipline is the domain's own: fluency is not understanding (the systems model the distribution of text, and their confident errors — 'hallucinations' — betray the difference); performance concentrates where data is (high-resource languages and registers — the corpus concept's representativeness limit at planetary scale, with most of the world's languages under-served); bias in training text becomes bias in systems; and evaluation needs linguistic instruments — the levels, the ambiguity types, the register and variation findings — to probe what fluent output conceals. Computational linguistics is thus the domain's mirror: every concept surveyed — phonology to pragmatics, acquisition to corpora — names both a capability the machines must reproduce and a test they must pass.
key_ideas: Computational linguistics/NLP inherits the levels tower as its task list — speech, morphology, tagging and parsing, sense disambiguation, dialogue, translation — with ambiguity as the unifying hard problem.

Three eras: rule-based systems (transparent, brittle; the early MT overpromise as cautionary tale), statistical NLP (probabilities from corpora; n-grams and HMMs), and neural models (embeddings, attention, large language models).
N-gram models predict from a fixed window and cannot see long-distance dependencies; recursion guarantees language contains them — a linguistic fact with architectural consequences.
Word embeddings geometrize the distributional hypothesis (Firth's 'company it keeps'): corpus collocation becomes vector proximity.
Large language models are next-word predictors over vast corpora: fluent and multi-task, but fluency is not understanding — confident errors reveal distribution-modelling beneath.
Evaluation is applied linguistics: data concentration (high-resource bias), encoded social bias, and ambiguity/register probes are the domain's findings turned into test instruments.

common_misconceptions.misconception: Computers process language by looking up dictionary meanings and applying grammar rules.
common_misconceptions.correction: That describes the rule-based era, which broke against real text's variety and ambiguity. Modern systems learn statistical and vector representations from corpora; their 'knowledge' is distributional patterning, not consulted definitions — which explains both their breadth and their characteristic failure modes.

common_misconceptions.misconception: A system that produces fluent, grammatical text understands language.
common_misconceptions.correction: Fluency demonstrates mastery of textual distribution, not comprehension: the same systems confidently generate false statements and miss inferences a child makes. The domain's own distinction applies — modelling the form of the signal is not possessing its meaning and use; evaluation must probe beneath surface fluency.

common_misconceptions.misconception: Language technology works equally well for all languages.
common_misconceptions.correction: Performance tracks training data, and data tracks wealth, population, and web presence: a handful of high-resource languages enjoy strong systems while thousands of languages — most of the world's linguistic diversity — are barely served. This is the corpus representativeness limit at global scale, and it is an equity issue as much as an engineering one.

common_misconceptions.misconception: Since machines now generate language, linguistics is obsolete — the data does the science.
common_misconceptions.correction: The dependency runs the other way: linguistic categories define the tasks (what is a parse, a sense, a register?), linguistic phenomena supply the hard cases (ambiguity, long-distance dependency, politeness), and linguistic analysis builds the evaluations that expose what fluency conceals. The better the systems, the more the probing needs the science.

visual_teaching_suggestions: A three-era timeline: rule-based, statistical, neural — each with its mechanism icon, signature success, and signature failure, the MT winter marked as the cautionary flag.
An n-gram horizon diagram: the specimen's long-distance dependency arcing far over a sliding three-word window that can never contain it.
A 2-D embedding-space sketch: the lock/key/bolt/gate cluster and the open/unlock neighbourhood contrast, labelled as 'collocation, geometrized' — with the probe-battery checklist as a closing panel.

worked_example_blueprint.setup: The running specimen 'Could you unlock the gate?' processed by three generations of system, plus two probe tasks: (1) a rule-based pipeline with hand-written grammar; (2) a trigram model trained on a small corpus; (3) a neural model with embeddings and attention; probes: the attachment ambiguity 'She watched the man with the telescope' and the indirect-request reading of the specimen itself. The task: trace each system's handling and evaluate with the domain's instruments.
steps: Run the rule-based trace: tokenize, tag ('could' modal, 'unlock' verb...), parse by hand-coded rules — transparent at every step; then break it: feed dialect input ('Could you unlock them gates?') and watch coverage fail — the brittleness that ended era one, and the sociolinguistics concept's variation as the breaking data.

Run the trigram view: the model scores 'unlock the gate' highly (frequent trigram) and prefers it over 'unlock the gaze' — real power from mere counts; then expose the horizon: in 'the gate that the rains the villagers had prayed for finally opened', the dependency between 'gate' and 'opened' spans past any trigram window — recursion's guarantee, met by architecture failure.

Inspect the embedding space: 'lock', 'key', 'bolt', 'gate' cluster; 'unlock' sits near 'open' but their neighbourhoods differ exactly as the corpus concept's collocation profiles differed — state the identity: distributional hypothesis, geometrized.

Probe the neural model with the attachment ambiguity: it resolves 'with the telescope' by learned plausibility (watching-instruments are telescope-like) — often humanlike, and wrong the same way humans garden-path when plausibility misleads; connect to psycholinguistics: the systems inherit the ambiguity problem, not just the tasks.

Probe pragmatics on the specimen: does the system respond to 'Could you unlock the gate?' with action-oriented content or a literal ability answer? — test the indirect speech act; note that handling it requires exactly the pragmatics concept's machinery, learned or engineered, and that failures here are failures of the highest floor of the levels tower.

Close with the evaluation brief: fluency observed everywhere, so probe instead — one ambiguity battery (syntax), one register/dialect battery (sociolinguistics), one low-resource check (corpus representativeness), one confident-error hunt (fluency ≠ understanding); verdict template: capabilities named, limits named, both with linguistic instruments — the domain closing as the toolkit it promised to be.
worked_example_blueprint.expected_outcome: The learner traces rule-based, statistical, and neural handling of one utterance, demonstrates the n-gram horizon against a recursion-built dependency, identifies embeddings as geometrized collocation, probes attachment and indirect-request handling, and assembles a linguistically instrumented evaluation brief.

item_types: Era-attribution task (match system behaviours and failure modes to rule-based, statistical, or neural approaches)
Limit-demonstration task (construct sentences exposing n-gram horizons, attachment ambiguity, or indirect speech acts, and predict system behaviour)
Evaluation-design task (build a small linguistically motivated probe battery for a described system, with each probe tied to a domain concept)
practice_blueprint.difficulty_progression: Begin with era attribution from described behaviours; progress to constructing limit-exposing inputs with predictions; end with full probe-battery design where every test cites the linguistic phenomenon it operationalizes.

assessment_blueprint.bloom_alignment: Apply — learners must use linguistic knowledge to predict, expose, and evaluate system behaviour, operating the domain's concepts as instruments rather than reciting the history.
assessment_blueprint.mastery_signal: The learner attributes behaviours to eras by mechanism, constructs inputs that isolate specific limits (window, attachment, indirectness), never accepts fluency as understanding, and designs probes that each name the linguistic phenomenon under test.

real_world_applications: Language technology is daily infrastructure — search, autocomplete, dictation, translation, and assistants — and its linguistically literate users are its most effective and safest users.
Evaluation and safety work on large language models is a growing profession whose core method is exactly this concept's: linguistically motivated probing of fluent systems.
Low-resource language technology (keyboards, recognizers, MT for under-served languages) is an active equity frontier joining documentation linguistics with NLP engineering.

source_references: Fromkin, Rodman & Hyams An Introduction to Language
Yule, G. The Study of Language
